text.title: Prompt Portability Audit — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `prompt-portability-audit` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a audit from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: The audit names the artefact set (paths, repos, IDs) before starting. Scope creep mid-audit is rejected.
rule.rationale: Unbounded audits never finish and erode trust. Fixing scope at the start makes 'done' a definable state.

rule.statement: Every finding cites the specific criterion it violates (rule id, policy line, schema field). Findings without a criterion are flagged as opinions.
rule.rationale: Findings without criteria collapse into debate. Criterion-tagged findings are defensible and reviewable.

rule.statement: Each finding has severity (low / medium / high), a remediation owner, and a deadline. Findings without owners are organisational debt.
rule.rationale: Unowned findings live in a doc forever. The owner+deadline is what converts an audit into change.

rule.statement: The audit packages evidence (queries, screenshots, code references) so a second auditor can reproduce the finding.
rule.rationale: Prompts often encode model-specific assumptions (Claude's XML, OpenAI's system messages). Need a methodology to audit a prompt suite for portability before cross-vendor migration.

rule.statement: Findings are tracked in a single backlog with explicit close conditions; closure is verified by the auditor, not the implementer alone.

rationale.source: ISACA audit-followup practice; SOC2 remediation discipline

rule.rationale: Self-attested closure is how audit findings reopen six months later. Auditor-verified closure breaks the loop.
